params.description: Scale of 1 option pulses briefly to acknowledge selection.

steps.text: Scale of 1 would take 70 tick marks to reach Ben's data. That's way too crowded. Look at the biggest number and think about how many lines you'd need.

params.description: Scale of 2 option pulses briefly to acknowledge selection.

steps.text: Scale of 2 would work if the numbers were small, but to reach 70, you'd need 35 tick marks. That's a lot. Try a bigger scale.

params.description: Scale of 5 option pulses briefly to acknowledge selection.

steps.text: Scale of 5 could fit the data, but to reach 70, you'd need 14 tick marks. There's a bigger scale that makes the graph even cleaner.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Visual comparison shows all four scales side by side. Scale of 1 shows 70 marks crowded together. Scale of 2 shows 35 marks. Scale of 5 shows 14 marks. Scale of 10 shows 8 marks, cleanest and easiest to read.

steps.text: Let me show you why Scale of 10 works best here. The biggest number is 70. Scale of 1 would need 70 tick marks. That's way too many. Scale of 2 needs 35. Still crowded. Scale of 5 needs 14. Better, but we can do even cleaner. Scale of 10 only needs 8 tick marks to reach 70. That's the clearest graph. When data goes up to 70 and all the numbers are multiples of 5, Scale of 10 is your best choice.
